title: IgA Nephropathy
oneLiner: Most common primary glomerulonephritis worldwide; linked to mucosal infections; key cause of progressive kidney disease in young adults.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 24-year-old man presents to the emergency department with tea-coloured urine. He says it started three days ago, and he noticed it after a bout of pharyngitis – his throat was sore last week, he recovered, then the urine turned dark. He has no flank pain, no fever now. He feels well apart from mild lower back ache. BP is 132/84 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows 3+ blood and 1+ protein.

- **Tea-coloured urine (macroscopic hematuria):** Suggests glomerular bleeding – note the timing: it happens *during* or *just after* a mucosal infection (synpharyngitic), not 2-3 weeks later (post-streptococcal). This temporal clue is pathognomonic.
- **Age 20-30:** Peak incidence in young adults; male predominance (~2:1).
- **Pharyngitis trigger:** Mucosal infection (especially upper respiratory) – the IgA response goes awry. Contrast with post-streptococcal GN which follows skin or throat infection by 1-3 weeks.
- **No flank pain or fever:** Hematuria is painless, though patients often complain of a dull ache – likely from capsular stretch.
- **Mild hypertension + low-grade proteinuria:** Already hints at active disease; even a small amount of protein carries prognostic significance.
- **Dipstick: blood + protein:** Glomerular origin – look for dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts on microscopy.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Incidental finding on routine urinalysis:** Asymptomatic microscopic hematuria with or without proteinuria – patient feels fine, no infection history. This is common; you’ll pick it up at insurance checks or pregnancy screens. Do not dismiss – it may be early IgA nephropathy.
- **Acute kidney injury with oliguria:** Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN) pattern – patient presents with rising creatinine, hematuria, proteinuria, and sometimes hypertension. This is a crescentic variant (~5% of cases) – needs urgent biopsy and treatment.
- **Nephrotic syndrome:** Gross edema, frothy urine, proteinuria >3.5 g/day. IgA nephropathy is not the first thought (minimal change disease is), but it can present this way, especially in children. Biopsy shows mesangial IgA deposits plus podocyte foot process effacement.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
IgA nephropathy (Berger’s disease) is a primary glomerulonephritis characterised by mesangial deposition of IgA immune complexes. It is the **most common primary glomerulonephritis worldwide**, accounting for 20–40% of all renal biopsies. It matters because:

- **Frequency:** Up to 50% of patients will progress to chronic kidney disease (CKD) over 20–30 years; ~15–40% reach end-stage renal disease (ESRD) requiring dialysis or transplant.
- **Stakes:** It’s a leading cause of ESRD in young adults – a patient you see today at 25 could be on dialysis by 50. Early identification and risk stratification are critical.
- **Common pitfalls:** Easily mistaken for post-streptococcal GN (timing is key), benign familial hematuria (thin basement membrane nephropathy), or Alport syndrome (family history + hearing loss). Missing the diagnosis delays proper management – especially blood pressure control and proteinuria reduction.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:** IgA nephropathy is an immune-complex–mediated disease. The inciting event is a **mucosal infection** (usually respiratory or gastrointestinal) that triggers an exaggerated IgA immune response. But this is not just any IgA – in patients with IgA nephropathy, the IgA1 subclass is **aberrantly glycosylated** (missing galactose in the hinge region). This abnormal IgA1 is poorly cleared by the liver, accumulates in circulation, and the body makes IgG autoantibodies against it. These immune complexes deposit in the glomerular mesangium – the supporting matrix between capillaries.

- **Why macroscopic hematuria?** Deposition triggers mesangial proliferation and complement activation (via the lectin pathway). This causes transient increases in glomerular capillary pressure and breaks in the filtration barrier. RBCs leak into the urine – grossly visible when >50,000–100,000/mL. The timing (within days of mucosal infection) is because the IgA response peaks around day 4-5, then deposits → inflammation → hematuria.
- **Why proteinuria?** Ongoing mesangial injury → podocyte stress → foot process effacement → increased permeability to albumin. Proteinuria is a marker of disease activity and predicts progression.
- **Why hypertension?** Glomerular inflammation → reduced GFR → salt and water retention → volume-dependent hypertension. Also, local intraglomerular hypertension from angiotensin II activation (due to low nephron mass) worsens injury – a vicious cycle.
- **Why is it progressive?** Repeated cycles of deposition and inflammation lead to mesangial cell proliferation, matrix expansion (focal segmental glomerulosclerosis – FSGS), and eventually global sclerosis and tubular atrophy. **The Oxford classification (MEST-C)** scores these lesions: mesangial hypercellularity (M), endocapillary hypercellularity (E), segmental sclerosis (S), tubular atrophy/interstitial fibrosis (T), and crescents (C). Higher scores predict faster progression.

**Connect to pattern recognition:**  
The young man with synpharyngitic hematuria has an acute flare – mesangial deposits with endocapillary proliferation (probably E1). His mild proteinuria and normal-ish BP are good signs. But if you ignore him and he gets recurrent episodes, the score climbs – sclerosis (S1), fibrosis (T1-2) – and you lose nephrons. That’s why early BP control and proteinuria reduction matter.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common) | Unknown – likely multifactorial genetic + environmental | No identifiable trigger; hematuria may be incidental or cyclical |
| **Infectious triggers** | Mucosal infections: URTI (pharyngitis, tonsillitis), GIT (gastroenteritis), urinary tract? Rarely | "Sore throat last week, now bloody urine" – the classic clue |
| **Autoimmune / Systemic** | Henoch-Schönlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) – same pathophysiology but with systemic vasculitis | Palpable purpura on legs, abdominal pain, arthralgia – then renal involvement (may be called HSP nephritis, but it's the same IgA deposition) |
| **Liver disease** | Cirrhosis (especially alcoholic) – impaired hepatic clearance of IgA | Known liver disease + hematuria/proteinuria; low C3? |
| **Celiac disease** | 2-5% of celiac patients have IgA nephropathy; gluten-free diet may resolve hematuria | Patient with celiac – even if undiagnosed – presenting with recurrent macroscopic hematuria |
| **HIV, chronic infections** | HIV-associated nephropathy may show IgA deposits, but often a different pattern | Known HIV or other chronic infection |
| **Medications** | Rifampicin, NSAIDs rarely | Temporal association with starting drug |

**Most common cause:** **Idiopathic** – over 90% of cases have no clear underlying disease. But triggers are almost always mucosal infections, especially of the upper respiratory tract.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnosis is confirmed by renal biopsy** – no other test is definitive. But the workup rules out mimics and assesses severity.

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Urinalysis (dipstick + microscopy)** | Hematuria, proteinuria; dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts | Screens for glomerular disease; RBC casts are pathognomonic for glomerular bleeding | >80% dysmorphic RBCs = glomerular origin; RBC casts = confirm glomerulonephritis |
| **Renal function (creatinine, eGFR)** | Baseline kidney function | Risk stratification; helps decide when to biopsy (e.g., if eGFR is dropping) | Normal or mildly reduced – good prognosis; rapid decline = crescentic variant |
| **Urine protein quantification** | 24-hour urinary protein or spot protein:creatinine ratio (PCR) | Assess severity of glomerular injury; guides therapy threshold | >0.5-1 g/day increases risk of progression; >1 g/day often triggers immunosuppression |
| **Serum complement C3, C4** | Low C3 in post-streptococcal GN and lupus; normal in IgA nephropathy | Rule out other causes (post-strep, lupus, MPGN) | **Normal C3/C4 supports IgA nephropathy** – not diagnostic, but helpful |
| **ANA, anti-dsDNA** | Lupus serology | Rule out lupus nephritis (which can also have IgA deposits) | Negative ANA makes lupus unlikely |
| **ANCA** | Anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies | Rule out microscopic polyangiitis, granulomatosis with polyangiitis (can cause RPGN with hematuria) | Negative ANCA supports IgA nephropathy over vasculitis |
| **Renal biopsy** | Light microscopy: mesangial proliferation, endocapillary hypercellularity, crescents, sclerosis. Immunofluorescence: **dominant/mesangial IgA deposits** (often with C3). Electron microscopy: mesangial electron-dense deposits | **Gold standard for diagnosis**; also gives prognostic score (Oxford MEST-C) | IgA deposition is pathognomonic – do not treat without biopsy except in obvious HSP with classic rash |
| **Blood cultures / throat swab** | If suspicion of post-streptococcal GN | Differentiate by confirming recent streptococcal infection (ASOT, anti-DNAse B) | Negative/static titers point away from PSGN |

**Single finding that confirms:** Mesangial IgA deposits on immunofluorescence of renal biopsy – nothing else does. But clinically, the **combination of synpharyngitic macroscopic hematuria + normal complements + IgA deposition on biopsy** is diagnostic.

**Single finding that rules out:** Absence of IgA on immunofluorescence (unless you missed the sample – always ensure biopsy contains sufficient glomeruli).

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**A. Immediate (first hour) – What + Why**
- **For all patients with macroscopic hematuria ± AKI:**
  - **Assess volume status and BP:** Check lying/standing BP, assess for edema. Why: volume overload worsens hypertension and AKI.
  - **Order stat labs:** Urea, creatinine, electrolytes, urine dip + microscopy – see above.
  - **If BP ≥140/90 mmHg:** Start an ACE inhibitor (e.g., **ramipril 2.5–5 mg PO once daily**) or ARB. Why: reduces proteinuria and slows GFR decline. If patient is euvolemic, this is first-line even if normotensive (if proteinuria >0.5 g/day).
  - **Advise on volume status:** If grossly edematous or hypertensive, consider **furosemide 20–40 mg IV** (single dose) to control BP and reduce kidney congestion – only in hospital.
  - **Stop nephrotoxins:** NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast (unless absolutely necessary). NSAIDs can worsen proteinuria and AKI.

**B. Ward (24–72 hours) – What + Why**
- **Confirm diagnosis:** Organise renal biopsy if not done (indications: persistent proteinuria >0.5 g/day, AKI, declining eGFR, or suspicion of crescentic disease). Urgent biopsy if RPGN or rapid rise in creatinine.
- **Start immunosuppression if indicated:**
  - Indications: persistent proteinuria >1 g/day despite 3-6 months of maximal supportive therapy, OR crescentic IgA nephropathy (≥50% crescents on biopsy), OR rapidly progressive course.
  - **High-dose steroids:** **Methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg IV daily × 3 days**, then **oral prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day** (max 60 mg/day) tapered over 6 months. Why: reduces inflammation and proteinuria in high-risk patients. Note: benefit is modest; risk of infection and weight gain.
  - **If steroid-dependent or high-risk:** Add **mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) 1000–2000 mg/day** or **cyclophosphamide** for life-threatening crescentic disease (consult renal specialist).
- **Check for secondary causes:** If no clear trigger, screen for celiac (anti-tTG IgA), HIV, liver disease. In HSP with rash, manage systemically with same approach.
- **Monitor renal function daily:** If rising creatinine, check for other causes (volume depletion, nephrotoxic drugs, crescentic transformation).

**Important: What NOT to do in the first 72 hours:**
- Do NOT use corticosteroids for low-risk IgA nephropathy (proteinuria <0.5 g/day, normal eGFR) – the risk outweighs benefit.
- Do NOT prescribe NSAIDs for pain – they reduce GFR and can precipitate acute-on-chronic kidney injury.
- Do NOT assume every case with hematuria and IgA on biopsy needs aggressive treatment – most are low-risk and need only BP control.

**C. Long-term – What + Why**
- **Supportive therapy is the backbone (for all patients):**
  - **ACEi or ARB** (target BP <130/80 mmHg, and/or <120/70 if proteinuria >1 g/day). Titrate to maximum tolerated dose. Why: renoprotective – reduces proteinuria and intraglomerular hypertension.
  - **SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin 10 mg daily) if eGFR ≥25 mL/min:** Recent data from DAPA-CKD and sub-analyses show reduced proteinuria and slowed GFR decline in IgA nephropathy (even if no diabetes).
  - **Salt restriction (<5 g/day):** Synergistic with ACEi for BP control.
  - **Protein restriction (0.8 g/kg/day) if CKD stage 3+:** Delay progression.
- **Immunosuppression for high-risk:** Continue steroid taper for 6 months, then reassess. Long-term immunosuppression rarely needed unless repeat biopsy shows active crescents or worsening proteinuria.
- **Monitor:** Urine PCR + creatinine every 3–6 months; BP every visit. Re-biopsy if rapid decline or to reassess histology (rare).
- **Renal replacement therapy:** When eGFR <10-15 mL/min, prepare for dialysis or transplant. IgA nephropathy recurs in ~30% of transplants, but long-term graft survival is still good.
- **Vaccinations:** Ensure pneumococcal and influenza – these patients are at risk of infection, especially if on immunosuppression.

**Drug doses inline summary:**
| Drug | Dose/Route | Duration |
|------|------------|----------|
| Ramipril | 2.5–10 mg PO once daily | Indefinite |
| Lisinopril | 5–40 mg PO once daily | Indefinite |
| Losartan | 25–100 mg PO once daily | Indefinite |
| Methylprednisolone | 500–1000 mg IV daily × 3 | Then switch to oral |
| Prednisolone | 0.5–1 mg/kg PO daily (max 60 mg) | Taper over 6 months |
| Dapagliflozin | 10 mg PO once daily | Indefinite if eGFR ≥25 |
| MMF | 500–1000 mg PO twice daily | 6–12 months (off-label) |
| Cyclophosphamide | 500–1000 mg IV monthly × 6 | For crescentic disease |

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | IgA Nephropathy | Post-streptococcal GN | Thin Basement Membrane Nephropathy | Alport Syndrome (X-linked) |
|---------|----------------|------------------------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain / presentation** | Synpharyngitic macroscopic hematuria (days after infection); often painless | Post-infectious (2-3 weeks after group A strep); acute nephritic syndrome (edema, hypertension, oliguria) | Incidental microscopic hematuria; often family history of benign hematuria | Progressive microscopic hematuria since childhood; sensorineural deafness; ocular abnormalities (lenticonus) |
| **Exam** | Usually normotensive; no edema (unless nephrotic) | Hypertensive, periorbital edema, oliguria | Normal | Often normal until late stages; hearing loss (high-frequency) |
| **Lab / biopsy** | IgA deposits in mesangium on immunofluorescence; normal complements | Granular IgG and C3 along capillary walls; low C3; positive ASOT or anti-DNAse B | Normal glomeruli on light microscopy; thin GBM (150-250 nm) on EM; no immune deposits | Irregular thinning and thickening of GBM, splitting of lamina densa; no IgA deposits; genetic testing (COL4A5) |
| **Key differentiator** | IgA deposits on IF | Low C3, recent strep infection, IgG deposits | EM shows thin GBM; no immune deposits | Family history, hearing/eye findings, X-linked inheritance |

**Additional mimics to consider:**
- **Lupus nephritis:** Can have mesangial IgA deposits, but also full house immunofluorescence (IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C1q) – not just IgA. Low C3/C4, ANA positive.
- **Membranoproliferative GN (MPGN):** Subendothelial deposits, low C3, C3 nephritic factor.
- **Rapidly progressive GN:** Pauci-immune (ANCA-associated) – no IgA deposits.

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Progressive CKD leading to ESRD:** The most feared complication. Presents with rising creatinine, worsening proteinuria, hypertension that becomes harder to control. What to do: escalate to ACEi/ARB to max tolerated, reassess for immunosuppression, prepare for dialysis/transplant.
- **Crescentic transformation (RPGN):** Presents as rapid rise in creatinine over weeks, oliguria/anuria, fever, hematuria. Key signs: eGFR drop >50% in <3 months, biopsy shows >50% crescents. What to do: urgent high-dose steroids + cyclophosphamide, consider plasma exchange if life-threatening. This is a **nephrology emergency** – call your registrar immediately.
- **Hypertension and cardiovascular disease:** Chronic kidney disease accelerates vascular aging. Missed or undertreated hypertension → left ventricular hypertrophy, stroke, MI. Monitor BP strictly.
- **Thromboembolism (if nephrotic syndrome):** If proteinuria >10 g/day, risk of renal vein thrombosis and PE. Prophylactic anticoagulation (warfarin or DOAC) if nephrotic and albumin <20 g/L.
- **Recurrence in transplant:** 30% of transplant recipients develop IgA deposits in allograft; 5-10% progress to graft loss. Hence living donor screening for IgA nephropathy is controversial.

**Late presentation pattern that tests "what happens if untreated":** A 40-year-old man with no medical follow-up presents with fatigue, foamy urine, blood pressure 170/100, creatinine 450 µmol/L (eGFR 12). He had recurrent hematuria since age 20 but never sought help. Biopsy shows global sclerosis in 60% of glomeruli, extensive interstitial fibrosis. No chance of recovery – he will need renal replacement therapy within months. This is a tragedy – early detection and ACEi could have delayed ESRD by decades.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic 1: IG-A – Indolent, Gross hematuria, Adolescents**  
- **I** – **I**ndolent onset (often silent)  
- **G** – **G**ross hematuria after mucosal infection  
- **A** – **A**dolescents and young adults  

**Mnemonic 2: SPIT – Signs to Prognosticate in IgA**  
- **S** – **S**ynpharyngitic (timing)  
- **P** – **P**roteinuria >1 g/day (bad)  
- **I** – **I**mpaired eGFR at diagnosis (bad)  
- **T** – **T**ubular atrophy on biopsy (bad)  

**Pearls (Pattern-Recognition Cues):**

1. **"Sore throat + blood in urine = think IgA nephropathy, not PSCN, unless there’s a 2-3 week lag."**  
2. **Proteinuria is the most important prognostic factor after eGFR.** Spot PCR >1 predicts progression.  
3. **Oxford MEST-C score is not just for pathologists – know the terms:** M (mesangial hypercellularity), E (endocapillary), S (sclerosis), T (tubular atrophy), C (crescents). T score dominates prognosis.  
4. **Do not biopsy every patient with isolated microscopic hematuria and normal eGFR.** If proteinuria <0.5 g/day and BP normal, you can monitor with urine PCR every 6 months. Biopsy only if proteinuria rises or function declines.  
5. **SGLT2 inhibitors are now standard of care for proteinuric IgA nephropathy** (dapagliflozin 10 mg daily) – they reduce proteinuria and slow GFR decline even without diabetes.  
6. **Recurrence after transplant occurs but treatment is the same – ACEi/ARB and watch; rarely need immunosuppression.**  
7. **Henoch-Schönlein purpura = systemic IgA vasculitis = same kidney disease.** In children with HSP, renal involvement (proteinuria, hematuria) predicts long-term renal risk – treat similarly.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**
- **Mistake #1:** Waiting 2 weeks after pharyngitis to diagnose post-strep GN while the patient has IgA nephropathy. **Fix:** Check timing – if hematuria starts during or days after infection, it’s IgA.  
- **Mistake #2:** Ordering ANA and complement and thinking a normal result rules out glomerulonephritis. IgA nephropathy has normal complements – if you see low C3, think PSGN, lupus, or MPGN.  
- **Mistake #3:** Starting high-dose steroids in a patient with proteinuria 0.5 g/day and normal eGFR. Unnecessary toxicity – supportive care first.  
- **Mistake #4:** Not checking urine PCR for at least 3 months after an episode. Many patients have transient proteinuria; persistent proteinuria changes management.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior or the renal registrar (within hours):**

- **Rapidly rising creatinine (AKI stage 2 or 3):** eGFR drop >30% in 24 hours, or creatinine doubling. Suspect crescentic IgA or superimposed ATN. Needs urgent nephrology consult for biopsy and possible pulse steroids.  
- **Oliguria <400 mL/24h** – possible RPGN.  
- **Severe hypertension (BP >180/110 mmHg)** not responding to initial ACEi – risk of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) or rapid kidney decline.  
- **Hyperkalemia (K+ >6.0 mmol/L)** – especially if on ACEi/ARB – stop ACEi, give calcium gluconate + insulin/dextrose.  
- **Signs of fluid overload with respiratory distress** (pulmonary oedema) – give furosemide IV, CPAP, call ICU.  

**When to call ICU / crash team:**

- **Any of the above plus GCS <13** – possible hypertensive encephalopathy or PRES.  
- **Urgent dialysis indications:** severe hyperkalemia (K+ >7.0 with ECG changes), severe acidosis (pH <7.1), uraemic pericarditis, pulmonary oedema not responsive to diuretics.  
- **NEWS2 >5 or lactate >4 mmol/L** – although rare in isolated IgA, if patient also has infection (e.g., post-biopsy sepsis or pneumonia from steroids).

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 score** ≥6 triggers immediate review.
- **Serum lactate** >4 mmol/L: consider sepsis or bowel ischaemia if patient has concomitant abdominal pain (HSP can cause intestinal vasculitis).
- **GCS <13 or new focal neurology** – call stroke team and renal senior.

**In summary:** IgA nephropathy is common, often subtle, but can destroy kidneys silently. Your job is to detect it early, risk-stratify with proteinuria and BP, and escalate when the horse is bolting. Keep this entry in your personal knowledge bank – it will save you and your patients.

## IgA Nephropathy

**One-liner:** Most common primary glomerulonephritis worldwide; linked to mucosal infections; key cause of progressive kidney disease in young adults.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 24-year-old man presents to the emergency department with tea-coloured urine. He says it started three days ago, and he noticed it after a bout of pharyngitis – his throat was sore last week, he recovered, then the urine turned dark. He has no flank pain, no fever now. He feels well apart from mild lower back ache. BP is 132/84 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows 3+ blood and 1+ protein.

- **Age 20-30:** Peak incidence in young adults; male predominance (~2:1).
- **Pharyngitis trigger:** Mucosal infection (especially upper respiratory) – the IgA response goes awry. Contrast with post-streptococcal GN which follows skin or throat infection by 1-3 weeks.
- **No flank pain or fever:** Hematuria is painless, though patients often complain of a dull ache – likely from capsular stretch.
- **Mild hypertension + low-grade proteinuria:** Already hints at active disease; even a small amount of protein carries prognostic significance.
- **Dipstick: blood + protein:** Glomerular origin – look for dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts on microscopy.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Incidental finding on routine urinalysis:** Asymptomatic microscopic hematuria with or without proteinuria – patient feels fine, no infection history. This is common; you’ll pick it up at insurance checks or pregnancy screens. Do not dismiss – it may be early IgA nephropathy.
- **Acute kidney injury with oliguria:** Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN) pattern – patient presents with rising creatinine, hematuria, proteinuria, and sometimes hypertension. This is a crescentic variant (~5% of cases) – needs urgent biopsy and treatment.
- **Nephrotic syndrome:** Gross edema, frothy urine, proteinuria >3.5 g/day. IgA nephropathy is not the first thought (minimal change disease is), but it can present this way, especially in children. Biopsy shows mesangial IgA deposits plus podocyte foot process effacement.

IgA nephropathy (Berger’s disease) is a primary glomerulonephritis characterised by mesangial deposition of IgA immune complexes. It is the **most common primary glomerulonephritis worldwide**, accounting for 20–40% of all renal biopsies. It matters because:

- **Frequency:** Up to 50% of patients will progress to chronic kidney disease (CKD) over 20–30 years; ~15–40% reach end-stage renal disease (ESRD) requiring dialysis or transplant.

- **Common pitfalls:** Easily mistaken for post-streptococcal GN (timing is key), benign familial hematuria (thin basement membrane nephropathy), or Alport syndrome (family history + hearing loss). Missing the diagnosis delays proper management – especially blood pressure control and proteinuria reduction.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:** IgA nephropathy is an immune-complex–mediated disease. But this is not just any IgA – in patients with IgA nephropathy, the IgA1 subclass is **aberrantly glycosylated** (missing galactose in the hinge region). This abnormal IgA1 is poorly cleared by the liver, accumulates in circulation, and the body makes IgG autoantibodies against it. These immune complexes deposit in the glomerular mesangium – the supporting matrix between capillaries.

- **Why macroscopic hematuria?** Deposition triggers mesangial proliferation and complement activation (via the lectin pathway). This causes transient increases in glomerular capillary pressure and breaks in the filtration barrier. RBCs leak into the urine – grossly visible when >50,000–100,000/mL. The timing (within days of mucosal infection) is because the IgA response peaks around day 4-5, then deposits → inflammation → hematuria.
- **Why proteinuria?** Ongoing mesangial injury → podocyte stress → foot process effacement → increased permeability to albumin. Proteinuria is a marker of disease activity and predicts progression.
- **Why hypertension?** Glomerular inflammation → reduced GFR → salt and water retention → volume-dependent hypertension. Also, local intraglomerular hypertension from angiotensin II activation (due to low nephron mass) worsens injury – a vicious cycle.

Higher scores predict faster progression.

The young man with synpharyngitic hematuria has an acute flare – mesangial deposits with endocapillary proliferation (probably E1). His mild proteinuria and normal-ish BP are good signs. But if you ignore him and he gets recurrent episodes, the score climbs – sclerosis (S1), fibrosis (T1-2) – and you lose nephrons. That’s why early BP control and proteinuria reduction matter.

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common) | Unknown – likely multifactorial genetic + environmental | No identifiable trigger; hematuria may be incidental or cyclical |
| **Infectious triggers** | Mucosal infections: URTI (pharyngitis, tonsillitis), GIT (gastroenteritis), urinary tract? Rarely | "Sore throat last week, now bloody urine" – the classic clue |
| **Autoimmune / Systemic** | Henoch-Schönlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) – same pathophysiology but with systemic vasculitis | Palpable purpura on legs, abdominal pain, arthralgia – then renal involvement (may be called HSP nephritis, but it's the same IgA deposition) |

| **Celiac disease** | 2-5% of celiac patients have IgA nephropathy; gluten-free diet may resolve hematuria | Patient with celiac – even if undiagnosed – presenting with recurrent macroscopic hematuria |
| **HIV, chronic infections** | HIV-associated nephropathy may show IgA deposits, but often a different pattern | Known HIV or other chronic infection |
| **Medications** | Rifampicin, NSAIDs rarely | Temporal association with starting drug |

**Most common cause:** **Idiopathic** – over 90% of cases have no clear underlying disease. But triggers are almost always mucosal infections, especially of the upper respiratory tract.

**Diagnosis is confirmed by renal biopsy** – no other test is definitive. But the workup rules out mimics and assesses severity.

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Urinalysis (dipstick + microscopy)** | Hematuria, proteinuria; dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts | Screens for glomerular disease; RBC casts are pathognomonic for glomerular bleeding | >80% dysmorphic RBCs = glomerular origin; RBC casts = confirm glomerulonephritis |

| **Serum complement C3, C4** | Low C3 in post-streptococcal GN and lupus; normal in IgA nephropathy | Rule out other causes (post-strep, lupus, MPGN) | **Normal C3/C4 supports IgA nephropathy** – not diagnostic, but helpful |

| **Renal biopsy** | Light microscopy: mesangial proliferation, endocapillary hypercellularity, crescents, sclerosis. Immunofluorescence: **dominant/mesangial IgA deposits** (often with C3). Electron microscopy: mesangial electron-dense deposits | **Gold standard for diagnosis**; also gives prognostic score (Oxford MEST-C) | IgA deposition is pathognomonic – do not treat without biopsy except in obvious HSP with classic rash |
| **Blood cultures / throat swab** | If suspicion of post-streptococcal GN | Differentiate by confirming recent streptococcal infection (ASOT, anti-DNAse B) | Negative/static titers point away from PSGN |

**Single finding that confirms:** Mesangial IgA deposits on immunofluorescence of renal biopsy – nothing else does. But clinically, the **combination of synpharyngitic macroscopic hematuria + normal complements + IgA deposition on biopsy** is diagnostic.

**Single finding that rules out:** Absence of IgA on immunofluorescence (unless you missed the sample – always ensure biopsy contains sufficient glomeruli).

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**A. Immediate (first hour) – What + Why**
- **For all patients with macroscopic hematuria ± AKI:**
  - **Assess volume status and BP:** Check lying/standing BP, assess for edema. Why: volume overload worsens hypertension and AKI.
  - **Order stat labs:** Urea, creatinine, electrolytes, urine dip + microscopy – see above.
  - **If BP ≥140/90 mmHg:** Start an ACE inhibitor (e.g., **ramipril 2.5–5 mg PO once daily**) or ARB. Why: reduces proteinuria and slows GFR decline. If patient is euvolemic, this is first-line even if normotensive (if proteinuria >0.5 g/day).
  - **Advise on volume status:** If grossly edematous or hypertensive, consider **furosemide 20–40 mg IV** (single dose) to control BP and reduce kidney congestion – only in hospital.
  - **Stop nephrotoxins:** NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast (unless absolutely necessary). NSAIDs can worsen proteinuria and AKI.

**B. Ward (24–72 hours) – What + Why**
- **Confirm diagnosis:** Organise renal biopsy if not done (indications: persistent proteinuria >0.5 g/day, AKI, declining eGFR, or suspicion of crescentic disease). Urgent biopsy if RPGN or rapid rise in creatinine.
- **Start immunosuppression if indicated:**
  - Indications: persistent proteinuria >1 g/day despite 3-6 months of maximal supportive therapy, OR crescentic IgA nephropathy (≥50% crescents on biopsy), OR rapidly progressive course.
  - **High-dose steroids:** **Methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg IV daily × 3 days**, then **oral prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day** (max 60 mg/day) tapered over 6 months. Why: reduces inflammation and proteinuria in high-risk patients. Note: benefit is modest; risk of infection and weight gain.
  - **If steroid-dependent or high-risk:** Add **mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) 1000–2000 mg/day** or **cyclophosphamide** for life-threatening crescentic disease (consult renal specialist).
- **Check for secondary causes:** If no clear trigger, screen for celiac (anti-tTG IgA), HIV, liver disease. In HSP with rash, manage systemically with same approach.
- **Monitor renal function daily:** If rising creatinine, check for other causes (volume depletion, nephrotoxic drugs, crescentic transformation).

**Important: What NOT to do in the first 72 hours:**
- Do NOT use corticosteroids for low-risk IgA nephropathy (proteinuria <0.5 g/day, normal eGFR) – the risk outweighs benefit.
- Do NOT prescribe NSAIDs for pain – they reduce GFR and can precipitate acute-on-chronic kidney injury.
- Do NOT assume every case with hematuria and IgA on biopsy needs aggressive treatment – most are low-risk and need only BP control.

**C. Long-term – What + Why**
- **Supportive therapy is the backbone (for all patients):**
  - **ACEi or ARB** (target BP <130/80 mmHg, and/or <120/70 if proteinuria >1 g/day). Titrate to maximum tolerated dose. Why: renoprotective – reduces proteinuria and intraglomerular hypertension.
  - **SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin 10 mg daily) if eGFR ≥25 mL/min:** Recent data from DAPA-CKD and sub-analyses show reduced proteinuria and slowed GFR decline in IgA nephropathy (even if no diabetes).
  - **Salt restriction (<5 g/day):** Synergistic with ACEi for BP control.
  - **Protein restriction (0.8 g/kg/day) if CKD stage 3+:** Delay progression.
- **Immunosuppression for high-risk:** Continue steroid taper for 6 months, then reassess. Long-term immunosuppression rarely needed unless repeat biopsy shows active crescents or worsening proteinuria.
- **Monitor:** Urine PCR + creatinine every 3–6 months; BP every visit. Re-biopsy if rapid decline or to reassess histology (rare).
- **Renal replacement therapy:** When eGFR <10-15 mL/min, prepare for dialysis or transplant. IgA nephropathy recurs in ~30% of transplants, but long-term graft survival is still good.
- **Vaccinations:** Ensure pneumococcal and influenza – these patients are at risk of infection, especially if on immunosuppression.

**Drug doses inline summary:**
| Drug | Dose/Route | Duration |
|------|------------|----------|
| Ramipril | 2.5–10 mg PO once daily | Indefinite |
| Lisinopril | 5–40 mg PO once daily | Indefinite |
| Losartan | 25–100 mg PO once daily | Indefinite |
| Methylprednisolone | 500–1000 mg IV daily × 3 | Then switch to oral |
| Prednisolone | 0.5–1 mg/kg PO daily (max 60 mg) | Taper over 6 months |
| Dapagliflozin | 10 mg PO once daily | Indefinite if eGFR ≥25 |
| MMF | 500–1000 mg PO twice daily | 6–12 months (off-label) |
| Cyclophosphamide | 500–1000 mg IV monthly × 6 | For crescentic disease |

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | IgA Nephropathy | Post-streptococcal GN | Thin Basement Membrane Nephropathy | Alport Syndrome (X-linked) |
|---------|----------------|------------------------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain / presentation** | Synpharyngitic macroscopic hematuria (days after infection); often painless | Post-infectious (2-3 weeks after group A strep); acute nephritic syndrome (edema, hypertension, oliguria) | Incidental microscopic hematuria; often family history of benign hematuria | Progressive microscopic hematuria since childhood; sensorineural deafness; ocular abnormalities (lenticonus) |
| **Exam** | Usually normotensive; no edema (unless nephrotic) | Hypertensive, periorbital edema, oliguria | Normal | Often normal until late stages; hearing loss (high-frequency) |
| **Lab / biopsy** | IgA deposits in mesangium on immunofluorescence; normal complements | Granular IgG and C3 along capillary walls; low C3; positive ASOT or anti-DNAse B | Normal glomeruli on light microscopy; thin GBM (150-250 nm) on EM; no immune deposits | Irregular thinning and thickening of GBM, splitting of lamina densa; no IgA deposits; genetic testing (COL4A5) |
| **Key differentiator** | IgA deposits on IF | Low C3, recent strep infection, IgG deposits | EM shows thin GBM; no immune deposits | Family history, hearing/eye findings, X-linked inheritance |

**Additional mimics to consider:**
- **Lupus nephritis:** Can have mesangial IgA deposits, but also full house immunofluorescence (IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C1q) – not just IgA. Low C3/C4, ANA positive.
- **Membranoproliferative GN (MPGN):** Subendothelial deposits, low C3, C3 nephritic factor.
- **Rapidly progressive GN:** Pauci-immune (ANCA-associated) – no IgA deposits.

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Progressive CKD leading to ESRD:** The most feared complication. Presents with rising creatinine, worsening proteinuria, hypertension that becomes harder to control. What to do: escalate to ACEi/ARB to max tolerated, reassess for immunosuppression, prepare for dialysis/transplant.
- **Crescentic transformation (RPGN):** Presents as rapid rise in creatinine over weeks, oliguria/anuria, fever, hematuria. Key signs: eGFR drop >50% in <3 months, biopsy shows >50% crescents. What to do: urgent high-dose steroids + cyclophosphamide, consider plasma exchange if life-threatening. This is a **nephrology emergency** – call your registrar immediately.
- **Hypertension and cardiovascular disease:** Chronic kidney disease accelerates vascular aging. Missed or undertreated hypertension → left ventricular hypertrophy, stroke, MI. Monitor BP strictly.
- **Thromboembolism (if nephrotic syndrome):** If proteinuria >10 g/day, risk of renal vein thrombosis and PE. Prophylactic anticoagulation (warfarin or DOAC) if nephrotic and albumin <20 g/L.
- **Recurrence in transplant:** 30% of transplant recipients develop IgA deposits in allograft; 5-10% progress to graft loss. Hence living donor screening for IgA nephropathy is controversial.

**Late presentation pattern that tests "what happens if untreated":** A 40-year-old man with no medical follow-up presents with fatigue, foamy urine, blood pressure 170/100, creatinine 450 µmol/L (eGFR 12). He had recurrent hematuria since age 20 but never sought help. Biopsy shows global sclerosis in 60% of glomeruli, extensive interstitial fibrosis. No chance of recovery – he will need renal replacement therapy within months. This is a tragedy – early detection and ACEi could have delayed ESRD by decades.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic 1: IG-A – Indolent, Gross hematuria, Adolescents**  
- **I** – **I**ndolent onset (often silent)  
- **G** – **G**ross hematuria after mucosal infection  
- **A** – **A**dolescents and young adults  

**Mnemonic 2: SPIT – Signs to Prognosticate in IgA**  
- **S** – **S**ynpharyngitic (timing)  
- **P** – **P**roteinuria >1 g/day (bad)  
- **I** – **I**mpaired eGFR at diagnosis (bad)  
- **T** – **T**ubular atrophy on biopsy (bad)  

**Pearls (Pattern-Recognition Cues):**

1. **"Sore throat + blood in urine = think IgA nephropathy, not PSCN, unless there’s a 2-3 week lag."**  
2. **Proteinuria is the most important prognostic factor after eGFR.** Spot PCR >1 predicts progression.  
3. **Oxford MEST-C score is not just for pathologists – know the terms:** M (mesangial hypercellularity), E (endocapillary), S (sclerosis), T (tubular atrophy), C (crescents). T score dominates prognosis.  
4. **Do not biopsy every patient with isolated microscopic hematuria and normal eGFR.** If proteinuria <0.5 g/day and BP normal, you can monitor with urine PCR every 6 months. Biopsy only if proteinuria rises or function declines.  
5. **SGLT2 inhibitors are now standard of care for proteinuric IgA nephropathy** (dapagliflozin 10 mg daily) – they reduce proteinuria and slow GFR decline even without diabetes.  
6. **Recurrence after transplant occurs but treatment is the same – ACEi/ARB and watch; rarely need immunosuppression.**  
7. **Henoch-Schönlein purpura = systemic IgA vasculitis = same kidney disease.** In children with HSP, renal involvement (proteinuria, hematuria) predicts long-term renal risk – treat similarly.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**
- **Mistake #1:** Waiting 2 weeks after pharyngitis to diagnose post-strep GN while the patient has IgA nephropathy. **Fix:** Check timing – if hematuria starts during or days after infection, it’s IgA.  
- **Mistake #2:** Ordering ANA and complement and thinking a normal result rules out glomerulonephritis. IgA nephropathy has normal complements – if you see low C3, think PSGN, lupus, or MPGN.  
- **Mistake #3:** Starting high-dose steroids in a patient with proteinuria 0.5 g/day and normal eGFR. Unnecessary toxicity – supportive care first.  
- **Mistake #4:** Not checking urine PCR for at least 3 months after an episode. Many patients have transient proteinuria; persistent proteinuria changes management.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call your senior or the renal registrar (within hours):**

- **Rapidly rising creatinine (AKI stage 2 or 3):** eGFR drop >30% in 24 hours, or creatinine doubling. Suspect crescentic IgA or superimposed ATN. Needs urgent nephrology consult for biopsy and possible pulse steroids.  
- **Oliguria <400 mL/24h** – possible RPGN.  
- **Severe hypertension (BP >180/110 mmHg)** not responding to initial ACEi – risk of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) or rapid kidney decline.  
- **Hyperkalemia (K+ >6.0 mmol/L)** – especially if on ACEi/ARB – stop ACEi, give calcium gluconate + insulin/dextrose.  
- **Signs of fluid overload with respiratory distress** (pulmonary oedema) – give furosemide IV, CPAP, call ICU.  

**When to call ICU / crash team:**

- **Any of the above plus GCS <13** – possible hypertensive encephalopathy or PRES.  
- **Urgent dialysis indications:** severe hyperkalemia (K+ >7.0 with ECG changes), severe acidosis (pH <7.1), uraemic pericarditis, pulmonary oedema not responsive to diuretics.  
- **NEWS2 >5 or lactate >4 mmol/L** – although rare in isolated IgA, if patient also has infection (e.g., post-biopsy sepsis or pneumonia from steroids).

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 score** ≥6 triggers immediate review.
- **Serum lactate** >4 mmol/L: consider sepsis or bowel ischaemia if patient has concomitant abdominal pain (HSP can cause intestinal vasculitis).
- **GCS <13 or new focal neurology** – call stroke team and renal senior.

**In summary:** IgA nephropathy is common, often subtle, but can destroy kidneys silently. Your job is to detect it early, risk-stratify with proteinuria and BP, and escalate when the horse is bolting. Keep this entry in your personal knowledge bank – it will save you and your patients.
